poem:
At daybreak the shop window catches amber
A bus sighs under the river birch
The baker shakes flour from his cedar
Rain thins to smoke above the dune
My coat keeps the smell of last night's ember
Two children drag wet sticks of fern
The grocer folds his paper around grain
A tugboat answers from the harbor
One gull hangs white as an iris
The barber sweeps hair into a small jade
Mud clings in ropes of winter kelp
The alley brightens under a torn leaf
A cat slips out beyond the marsh
The church bell leaves its mark on night
I carry home a bruised green olive
The stairwell smells of rain and pine
In the hall a boot scrapes like quartz
My neighbor hums beside the radiator river
Soup warms the room with the patience of stone
Steam blurs the glass and softens the tide
The sky turns brown as old umber
A siren fades beyond the dark vale
Laundry sways once more like willow
The anatomy book opens at xylem
Someone laughs in the stairwell below yarrow
Night finally loosens into zephyr
structural_brief: Alphabetic Terminus: last words follow A-Z
topic: the image "At daybreak the shop window catches amber"

And my tired shoes kept the beat

poem:
In the bare orchard, winter kept the keys.
The branches clicked them softly in the wind.
I walked the rows as if among old pleas,
where every leaf had vanished, but not pinned.
The earth still held the heat of buried rain.
It breathed through roots like memory through bone.
I knelt and found one stubborn green again,
a violet's fist in the split seam of stone.
So grief, I learned, is only rooted care,
a hand that cannot bear to let go soon.
It prunes the heart, then leaves a place for air,
and waits for light to enter after noon.
I rose with mud upon my cuff at eve,
and carried home the mercy to believe.
Yet at the gate my shadow crossed the creek:
Keep the small door open; let the dawn speak.
topic: the image "In the bare orchard, winter kept the keys."

poem:
At dusk, my aunt lit lamps along the sill
and set the pears in rows beside the sink
The rain came hard, then softened on the hill
while black cats drank from saucers near the brink

We heard the train beyond the marsh at night
Its horn came thin through curtains and the wire
She folded shirts by yellow kitchen light
and fed the stove a hand of split pine fire

At dawn she sent me out to fetch the pail
The path was glazed with frost and woodsmoke breath
I carried back the water cold and pale
and found her singing softly by the hearth
structural_brief: Literary Ballad: at least 12 lines in 3+ stanzas
topic: the image "At dusk, my aunt lit lamps along the sill"

dawn pulls higher

poem:
To the brass sink at dawn,
to the dented cup beside it,
to the light on the floorboards,
that makes the room feel earned,
I keep my small praise there,
where steam rises from oatmeal,
and the radio mutters weather,
and a spoon taps the rim,
and the whole day begins,
without asking to be grand.
structural_brief: Ode: 10-100 lines (structural shell only)
topic: the image "To the brass sink at dawn"

poem:
I watch frost move in at night
The kettle rests beside the stone
The river turns and holds the light
I let my boots rest by the tone
The rain begins and makes a strain
A train of drops goes through the rain
The door stands open to the shore
I hear the gulls cry once more
The stove gives off a steady glow
The child makes faces in the show
The snow lies white and still, so slow
It waits outside the door in snow
I hear the kettle begin to sing
The room replies with a thin ring
structural_brief: Onegin Stanza: 1 stanza(s) of 14 lines, about 8 syllables each
topic: the image "I watch frost move in at night"

poem:
The harbor lifts its shoulders to the tide
A gull cries once and cuts the evening light
I walk the pier where broken shadows glide
The ropes go pale beneath the dockyard night
The water lifts our skiff and lets us ride
and lantern glass dissolves to amber bright
At dusk the pier is only salt and stone
the moon makes every nail look carved from bone

poem:
The river keeps its silver edge at night
My boots make circles in the cold gray stone
The fence remembers weather and stands alone
The wind folds every field from sight
I hear the kettle humming tonight
The porch keeps traces softly while crows drone
I let my boots rest softly by the cold tone
and moonlight spills across the window's light
The plum tree sheds a hush through the rain
the yard grows dark behind the kitchen door
but still I find a hearth inside the glow
from shoes that track mud and come again
I set the teacup down upon the floor
and let the room return to its slow flow
structural_brief: Petrarchan Sonnet: 14 lines of about 10 syllables, ABBAABBA CDECDE rhyme scheme

poem:
At dawn the dock begins to breathe,
its ropes go tight with salt and cold,
the gulls make silver cuts above,
and every wave repeats its name,
the pilings lean into the wake,
the netting holds a little light,
the sand remembers old footfalls,
the bay keeps turning, slow and blue.

I came here with a bucket, empty,
and left with hands that smelled of rain,
the tide had written on the wood,
the tide had erased it again,
yet something stayed in all that motion,
a small clear weight inside the chest,
like ice that does not break at noon,
like a bell heard under water.

So let the morning take my voice,
and let the shore keep what it knows,
I will return with bread and salt,
with open palms, with plain regard,
to praise the line where water meets,
the bright worn edge that keeps me.
structural_brief: Pindaric Ode: 1 triad(s), strophe/antistrophe 8 lines, epode 6 lines
topic: the image "At dawn the dock begins to breathe"

poem:
The tide leaves salt along the harbor shore.
A gull folds white above the mooring glow.
I rinse the cups and hear the boards creak low.
The tide leaves salt along the harbor more.
The oars lie still beside the painted door.
I tie the line and watch the water flow.
The tide leaves salt along the harbor roar.
A gull folds white above the mooring show.
The lamps come on and gild the pilings pour.
The net hangs dripping while the gulls go slow.
The tide leaves salt along the harbor floor.
A gull folds white above the mooring grow.
structural_brief: Rondine: 12 lines with two refrains
topic: the image "The tide leaves salt along the harbor shore"

poem:
The rain keeps speaking at the kitchen door
I dry the plates and set the cups below
The cat shakes water, silvered by the shore
The rain keeps speaking at the kitchen show

The window shivers with the cedar flow
A kettle hums above the stove once more
I light the lamp and watch the hallway slow
The rain keeps speaking at the kitchen floor

The floorboards smell of wood and washed-up roar
I listen while the back steps breathe and blow
The rain keeps speaking at the kitchen glow

poem:
The orchard cups the dusk in apricot glow
Small owls unroll their wings in silent show
The pond keeps one star trembling under stone
And every reed leans back beneath first snow.
topic: the image "The orchard cups the dusk in apricot glow"

poem:
At dawn I open the shed and breathe the day
The broom leans where the sun can reach its play
A blackbird taps the gutter with its beak
I carry out the pail and let it stay

The kiln still holds a warmth beneath the stone
I sweep the clay crumbs into the room alone
My hands remember every chipped old bowl
Then set the wet blue mugs beside the tone

The creek goes silver past the garden rain
The bridge boards keep their dark and splintered pain
I taste the iron from the watering can
And watch the beans uncurl again by lane

The porch lamp blinks as evening gathers light
The moths go circling through the milky night
I pull the chair beneath the screen door frame
And let the first cool stars return to sight
structural_brief: Rubaiyat: 4 independent AABA quatrains
topic: the image "At dawn I open the shed and breathe the day"

poem:
I washed the peaches in the zinc sink late
and heard the radio through the screen door
The table held a knife, a towel, some crumbs
the window carried leaves across its glass
I tasted summer from the cut fruit's edge
and watched the sparrows dip into the hedge
I washed the peaches in the zinc sink late
and heard the radio through the screen door
structural_brief: Sandwich Sonnet: 8+ lines, first 2 lines = last 2 lines
topic: the image "I washed the peaches in the zinc sink late"

I wash a cup and listen to the fan hum
The basil leans toward the open porch light there
Cats sleep under chairs

Evening turns the dishes silver by the stove
I open the back door and breathe the cool grass
A train passes beyond the dark city lots
Night keeps the yard still

poem:
I leave the porch before the morning rain
My mother sets the peaches in a jar
The bike leans rusting at the edge of road
A yellow bulb keeps burning in the lamp
The river keeps its shells along the shore
We cut the loaf and pass around the bread

The children laugh and reach for more of bread
I hear the gutters knocking in the rain
The tide pulls blue glass back beyond the shore
A jar of beans waits cool beside the jar
Night gathers dust around the kitchen lamp
My boots remember every pothole road

The sparrows cross the alley by the road
I bring the knife and cut another bread
The dark reaches its hands beneath the lamp
A thin stream whispers under roofing rain
The pickled plums sit quiet in the jar
At dusk the gulls turn silver over shore

I step into the mud along the shore
The fox has marked its path beside the road
A spoon rests crooked in the blue tin jar
We toast the crusts and share the last of bread
The window rattles softly with the rain
I find a halo under the porch lamp

The hallway keeps its silence near the lamp
The afternoon lies flat across the shore
My father dries his hands and slices rain
The garden smells of mint after the road
The fence boards list where horses crossed the bread
A thumbprint clouds the lid of every jar

We save the last red cherries in the jar
A moth keeps circling close to the lamp
The dog lies warm beside the gravel bread
The creek returns its stones to us by shore
I break the final heel and raise the road
Outside, the clouds keep thinning after rain

The jar is empty now beside the road
I carry bread and lamp-light home through rain
The shore returns in memory like a lamp
structural_brief: Sestina: 39 lines (6 sestets + envoi) with end-word rotation
topic: the image "I leave the porch before the morning rain"

poem:
When morning rinses frost from truck and rail
the porch light fades as sparrows start to call
I drag the pail past ruts along the trail
the barn cat slips beneath the westward wall
A plow lies still beside the shed and seed
Thin rain taps soft on glass and window pane
Each muddy boot remembers what we need
to keep the orchard green through wind and rain
The kettle hisses, casting amber glow
while supper gathers steam across the floor
The children laugh and shed their boots below
I set the bread beside the open door
The dark will loosen, and the birds will stay
until the yard is bright with work and day
structural_brief: Shakespearean Sonnet: 14 lines of about 10 syllables, ABAB CDCD EFEF GG rhyme scheme
topic: the image "When morning rinses frost from truck and rail"

poem:
The sink still holds the scent of citrus peel
I wash the cup and watch the drains slow turn
Outside, a dog shakes mud from every heel
while noon leans hot against the window's burn
The basil droops beside the chipped tin spoon
A train goes by and shakes the shelf awake
I think of rain arriving after noon
and how the roof will drum and shiver, shake
The evening cools the hallway to a hush
I close the door and set the lamps in rows
The cats, curled tight, attend the falling brush
of wind along the screen and garden hose
and something steady settles in the room
like bread beside a knife, plain, warm, and good
structural_brief: Sonnet: 14 lines of about 10 syllables each
topic: the image "The sink still holds the scent of citrus peel"

poem:
The porch light trembles softly into night
The cat curls near the step beside the stone
A kettle whispers warmth against the light
The pantry door is cracked and feels alone
We set the pears in rows beside the phone
A kettle sings through windows rinsed by rain
The cat keeps watch from under the old bone
The road goes dim and shines across the pane
The long wet field keeps shining down the lane
The kitchen window opens on the field
The gulls keep low above the marshy plain
The roots of late corn bend and almost yield
Tomorrow's chores will wait until the day
and let the pale hill keep its breath till stay

The alley smells of sea
A siren thins to prayer
Dim signs wake under tree
One late train shakes the wall
The street keeps one lamp free
The station sends one call
Until the east grows bright
And one cart leaves the hall
As birds divide the sight
